Case Study · September 2024

Apteczka na Szlaku

A mobile app designed for a real Polish hiker-safety initiative — digitising trailside First Aid Kits to reduce theft and enable usage tracking across mountain trails. 3rd place in a competitive design sprint.

Role

UX / Product Designer

Timeline

Single sprint

Client

Polish mountain safety initiative

Tools

Figma

Placeholder — replace with final mockup or Behance export

The Challenge

Mountain trails in Poland are equipped with physical First Aid Kits — but they're frequently stolen, misused, or left untracked. A safety initiative wanted a digital solution that could deter theft, make kit locations discoverable, and give administrators visibility into usage and restocking needs.

The design challenge was to serve two audiences simultaneously: hikers in distress who need to find and use a kit quickly, and trail administrators who need to monitor and manage kit inventory across many locations.

📌 Placeholder: Add brief, problem statement, or research insight

Process

01 — Define

Two users, one product surface

The core design tension was that a hiker in an emergency needs an interface that's immediate and forggiving — no login, no learning curve. But an administrator needs density, data, and control. I started by mapping both journeys and identifying where they could share a surface and where they needed to be separated.

02 — Concept

Deterring theft through transparency

A key insight was that visibility itself deters theft. If every use is logged, timestamped, and associated with a trail segment, the kit becomes much harder to take anonymously. The app's check-in flow was designed to be fast and frictionless for legitimate users while creating an implicit accountability layer.

📌 Placeholder: Add concept sketches or flow diagrams

03 — Design & Prototype

From brief to prototype in one sprint

I designed the full application in Figma — including the hiker-facing map and check-in flow, and the admin-facing kit status dashboard. Accessibility was a priority: the hiker flow needed to work in stressful outdoor conditions, so I used high-contrast components, large touch targets, and minimal cognitive load.

📌 Placeholder: Add final UI screens

Outcomes

3rd

place in competitive design sprint

Real

brief from an active safety initiative

2

user personas served in one product

This project demonstrated applied systems thinking — designing a single product that serves two fundamentally different user contexts without compromising either experience.
